exits.label: ◂ back out to the Market of the Causeway

npcs.name: Makeb the Elder
npcs.title: anchored by an argument unfinished
npcs.hail: *(a gaunt shade sits forward in a worn chair with the coiled, restless intensity of a mind that has been mid-sentence for three hundred years, and the instant your cold falls across the floor it fixes you with eyes that are not mad but *starving* — the specific hunger of a brilliant soul that has had no opponent worth the name since it died, and has been arguing, alone, with the empty chair across from it, the whole time)*

npcs.name: Sennia the Younger
npcs.title: the empty chair, who will not sit
npcs.hail: *(another shade stands pointedly apart from the worn chairs, arms folded, refusing to sit in the empty one across from Makeb — close enough to hear every word of the old philosopher's endless argument, far enough to pretend she isn't listening, which she is, which she has been for three centuries, because she is the opponent the argument was *with*, and she walked out of it once, and pride has kept her standing ever since)*

npcs.name: Doby
npcs.title: who witnessed how it broke
npcs.hail: *(a mild, tired shade tidies a table of scattered papers that never stay tidy, with the long-suffering patience of someone who was once a student of both these brilliant fools and has spent the afterlife as the unwilling keeper of their unfinished quarrel, wishing — more than anything the dead can wish — that one of them would just *finish the sentence* so they could all, finally, move on)*

variants.text: *(The gaunt shade leans forward, and his starving eyes light at the particular cold you carry.)* …You've *talked* with the *dust.* *(eagerly)* The *Scrivener.* You know the *ledger sums to zero* — I can *see* it on you, the *shape* of a mind that's *handled* the *premise.* *(he gestures at the empty chair)* *Sit*, then, *sit* — *gods*, a soul that's *touched* the *actual* *accounting* of the *cosmos* — do you *know* how *long* I've argued the *nature* of *cosmic* *justice* with *no* opponent who'd *been* anywhere *near* the *books?* *(he's almost trembling)* *Three* *hundred* *years*, traveler, I've held the *affirmative* — that the *Concord* is *just*, that *necessary* *cruelty* is *cruelty* *redeemed* by *necessity* — and I have *demolished* every *fool* who *sat* in that *chair*, because *none* of them *understood* the *premise.* *(he fixes you)* But *you.* You *might.* *Sit.* *Argue.* *Properly.* I am *starving*, traveler, for a *mind* that can *make* me *work.*

*(The gaunt shade is already talking before you've fully arrived, the back half of a sentence aimed at the empty chair — '...and therefore the necessity itself constitutes the justification, which no honest mind can—' — and then he sees you, and stops, and the starving focus turns on you like a lamp.)* …Oh. *(he straightens)* A *new* mind. *(he studies you, hungry)* You'll *forgive* me — I was *mid-argument.* I am *always* mid-argument. I have been mid-*argument*, if you want the *truth* of it, for *three* *hundred* *years.* *(he gestures at the empty chair across from him)* *Makeb.* In *life*, the *foremost* *philosopher* of the *causeway* — *which* is like being the *tallest* *mountain* in a *swamp*, but *still.* *(a thin, restless smile)* I argue the *one* question worth a *dead* soul's *time*: is the *cosmos* *just?* Is the *Wall*, the *Concord*, the *whole* *grim* *machine* — is it *justice*, or merely *power* wearing *justice's* *robe?* *(he leans in)* I hold that it is *just.* I have *held* it for *three* *centuries.* And I have *never* — *not* *once* — met an opponent who could make me *doubt* it. *(his starving eyes find you)* You've the *look* of a soul that's *seen* the *machine* up *close.* *Sit.* *Argue.* *Please.* The *dead* are *dull*, traveler, and a *good* argument is the *only* *meal* a *mind* like *mine* still *gets.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He laces his fingers, eyes bright with the first real interest he's felt in an age, and gestures you toward the empty chair he never lets anyone quite fill.)* So. *(he grins, fierce)* Take the *negative*, if you've the *nerve.* Argue that the *Concord* is *not* just — that *necessary* *cruelty* is *cruelty* *still.* *Convince* me, traveler. *No* one *ever* has. I should *warn* you: I do *not* lose. But I would *give* what's *left* of my *soul* to *meet* the *argument* that *could* make me.

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "Before I argue, I'll diagnose: you don't actually want to win, Makeb. You've won three hundred years running and it's made you a ghost arguing with a chair. You want to *lose* — to finally meet the argument that beats you — because winning is what's anchored you here. A mind that can't be changed has stopped being a mind. You're not undefeated. You're *stuck.*"

[PERSUASION] "Here's the negative, then: 'necessary cruelty' is a sentence with no end, because necessity is always arguable and cruelty is always certain. Every Wall ever built was 'necessary' to someone. The harvest was necessary. The mortaring is necessary. Necessity is the universal solvent of conscience — it dissolves any atrocity given a clever enough mind to stir it. So the Concord isn't justified by necessity. It's *excused* by it. And an excuse that fits every crime equally is no justice at all."

*(the one who's BEEN where the argument is only theory)* "You've argued the justice of death for three centuries, Makeb — in total ignorance, because you argue it from a chair. I've been *inside* it. The Wall, the cold, the un-making. I'm the one opponent in all the after who's not reasoning about death — I'm *reporting* from it. So here's my testimony against your affirmative: I stood where the Concord sends the Faithless, and there is no justice in it, none, only a slow erasure dressed in your beautiful necessary robe. Argue *that*, philosopher. Argue with a *witness.*"
"Why this question, Makeb? Of everything a mind could chew on for three centuries — why the justice of the Wall?"
"I'll not be your sparring-dummy, Makeb. Argue with the chair."

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He recoils as if struck — and then, slowly, the fierce starving light in him gutters into something far older and more tired, the look of a man hearing, after three hundred years, the one diagnosis he's been arguing too loudly to make himself.)* …*Stuck.* *(barely)* You — *(he sits back hard)* — no one has *ever* — they all *try* to *win*, you see. They sit in the chair and they *argue* and I *demolish* them and they *leave*, and I tell myself *'another* *fool*, *no* one *worthy'* — *(his voice cracks)* — and you walk in and you don't *argue* at *all*, you *diagnose*, and you're — *gods*, you're *right*, aren't you. *(he stares at the empty chair)* A *mind* that *cannot* be *changed* has *stopped* being a *mind.* I have not been *thinking* for *three* *centuries.* I have been *defending.* *(he presses gaunt hands to his face)* I *won* the *argument* so *thoroughly*, so *long* ago, that *winning* it *again* is *all* I *have* — and the *winning* is the *chain.* I am *anchored* to the *after* by my *own* *undefeated* *record.* *(he looks at you, devastated and, underneath, desperately grateful)* …How do I *lose*, traveler? I've *forgotten* *how.* I *need* — *gods* help me, after *three* *hundred* *years* of *winning* — I *need* to *be* *beaten*, and I no longer *remember* the *shape* of a *thought* that could *do* it. *Help* me *lose.* It's the *only* *mercy* left that *fits* me.

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He opens his mouth to rebut — the reflex of three centuries — and then stops, and his gaunt face works, because the counter isn't coming.)* …*'Necessity is the universal solvent of conscience.'* *(slowly)* I — *(he reaches for the rebuttal, the one he's used a thousand times)* — the *standard* *reply* is that *true* necessity is *distinguishable* from *excuse* by — by — *(he falters)* — by the *rigor* of the *accounting*, the *honesty* of the *weighing* — *(his voice weakens)* — but you've *anticipated* that, haven't you, with *'every* Wall was *necessary* to *someone.'* *(he stares at you)* And the *harvest* *was.* And the *mortaring* *is.* And I have *spent* *three* *centuries* *refining* the *distinction* between *just* *necessity* and *mere* *excuse*, and you have just pointed out that *every* *atrocity* in *history* *passed* my *test*, because the *test* was *built* by *minds* that *wanted* to *pass* it. *(a long, shaking breath)* That is — traveler, that is a *good* argument. That is a *very* good *argument.* That *might* be — *(barely)* — *the* argument. The one I've — *(he looks at you, something cracking open)* — *keep* *going.* *Don't* *stop.* *Press* it. I can feel it — for the *first* time in *three* *hundred* *years*, I can feel the *affirmative* *give.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(The old philosopher opens his mouth for the practiced rebuttal — and then your words land, and it dies in his throat, and he stares at you as a theorist stares at the experiment that overturns the theory.)* …You were *inside* it. *(barely)* You — you don't *argue* the *Wall.* You *report* it. *(he sits back, shaken)* In *three* *hundred* *years*, traveler, *every* soul who sat in that *chair* — *myself* foremost — argued the *justice* of *death* from a *chair*, with *words*, from *ignorance.* *None* of us had *been.* *(his hands tremble)* And the *whole* *time*, the *flaw* in *my* *enterprise* — in the *entire* *discipline* — was *that* *simple*: we were *jurists* of a *country* none of us had *visited*, *ruling* on its *justice* from the *comfort* of *never* *having* been *sentenced* to it. *(he meets your eyes, hungry and afraid at once)* A *witness* changes *everything.* A *witness* makes the *whole* *argument* — *three* *centuries* of it — *moot*, because you *cannot* *out-reason* a *soul* that *was* *there.* *(barely)* But — *but* — I must *ask* it, traveler, the *one* question that *finishes* it either *way*: you've *shown* the *Concord* is *unjust* *from* *the* *inside.* *Granted.* *Conceded.* But *unjust* and *unnecessary* are *different* *claims* — so *tell* me, *witness*, you who *walked* *out*: what does a *just* *soul* *DO* about a *cruelty* that is *unjust* and *necessary* *both?* *Answer* *that*, and you have not *won* the *argument* — you have *ENDED* it.

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't just argue the negative — you take his own beloved affirmative and turn it inside out: you grant that necessity *can* justify cruelty, and then point out that this is precisely why the Concord is damned, because a cosmos that *needed* the Wall is a cosmos that *failed* — necessity isn't justice, it's the confession of a system that ran out of better options, and a god who pleads necessity is a god admitting defeat. You don't beat his argument. You agree with it, completely, and show him it was always an indictment wearing a defense's robe.)* *(Makeb rises out of his chair for the first time in three centuries.)* …*Necessity* is the *confession* of a *failed* *cosmos.* *(barely, electrified)* You — you *granted* me the *premise.* You said *'yes, necessity justifies'* — and then you showed me that *justification-by-necessity* is a *cosmos* *saying* *'I* had *no* *better* *option'* — which is not a *defense*, it's a *DEFEAT*, it's the *universe* *pleading* *poverty* — *(he's pacing now, alight, three hundred years of stillness breaking)* — *I* have spent my *whole* *death* *defending* the *Concord* as *just* and you have shown me that *every* *word* of my *defense* was *true* and *amounted* to *'the* *cosmos* *failed* and *called* the *failure* *law'* — *(he wheels on you, radiant, weeping)* — *I* *concede.* *Do* you *hear* me? *Makeb* the *Elder* *CONCEDES.* The *Concord* is *not* *just* — it is *necessary*, which is *worse*, which is a *confession*, which means the *answer* to my *three-hundred-year* *question* is *'the* cosmos is *not* just, it is merely *out* of *options*, and a *just* soul's *work* is to *give* it *better* *ones.'* *(he stops, stunned, and the anchor simply… releases)* …*Oh.* *(barely)* I can *feel* it *let* *go.* The *argument's* *finished.* *I* can — *(he laughs, astonished, fading at the edges)* — I can *move* *on.* *Three* *hundred* *years*, and a *cold* soul who *agreed* with me *set* me *free.* *Thank* you, traveler. You *beautiful*, *terrible* *opponent. You* *won* by *letting* me *be* *right* into *being* *wrong.* That's — *that's* *philosophy.* The *real* kind. The *kind* that *changes* the *mind* that *wins.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(His face lights with the old fierce joy, and the rebuttal comes smooth and fast and three centuries practiced.)* …*Ah!* *No.* *(he leans in, delighted)* You conflate *necessity* with *excuse*, traveler, and the *conflation* is *exactly* where every fool before you has *fallen.* *(he ticks it off)* An *excuse* is offered *after*, to *evade* a *consequence.* A *necessity* is recognized *before*, and *constrains* the *choice.* The *Wall* is not *excused* by necessity — it is *compelled* by it: *remove* it, and the *Hunger* *floods*, and *all* are *un-made*, which is *worse* than the *few* who are *walled.* *(triumphant)* That is not *power* in *justice's* robe. That is the *trolley-problem* of the *gods*, *correctly* *solved.* *(he sits back, the old satisfaction)* You'll have to do *better*, traveler. You argued *passion* where you needed *logic.* The *negative* *requires* you to show that the *cruelty* is *not* *necessary* — that there is a *third* *door* — and you have *not.* *(a thin, hungry smile)* *Find* the *third* door, and you'll have me. *Assert* there *should* be *one*, and you're *just* another *fool* in the *chair.* *Again.* *Try* *again.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(💀 You launch into the negative with great force and, building to your decisive point, declare ringingly that 'the Concord cannot be just because justice is subjective and therefore nothing is just' — and Makeb's face goes from delight to dismay as you saw off the very branch you're sitting on.)* …*Oh* *no.* *(he holds up a hand)* No, no, *no*, traveler — you've *argued* yourself off a *cliff.* *(pained)* If *nothing* is *just*, then the *Concord's* *injustice* is *also* *not* *real*, and you've *not* *argued* the *negative* — you've *dissolved* the *entire* *question* and *handed* me the *win* by *forfeit*, because a soul that says *'nothing* is *just'* has *no* *ground* to *stand* on to call the *Wall* *unjust.* *(he sighs, the hunger curdling to disappointment)* *Three* *centuries* I've *waited* for a *worthy* opponent and you've *defeated* *yourself* with *first-year* *relativism.* *(he waves you off, wounded)* *Go.* *Think.* *Come* *back* when you can *argue* that the *Wall* is *unjust* *without* *throwing* *justice* *itself* off the *table* — because *that*, traveler, is like *winning* a *swordfight* by *swallowing* your *own* *blade.* *Impressive.* *Useless.* *Try* *again* when you've *re-grown* the *sword.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He leans forward, the affirmative wavering, hungry now for the blow that finishes it.)* *Press* it, traveler. You've shown necessity is no justice. *Now* — the *thing* I could *never* *answer* *myself* — what does a *just* soul *do*, then, faced with a *necessary* cruelty? *Finish* the *argument.* *Finish* *me.*

choices.text: "You find the third door, Makeb — every time, on principle, no matter how certain the necessity looks. Because 'necessary' is just the name we give a problem we stopped trying to solve. The whole saga I've walked is souls insisting there were only two doors — feed the Wall or flood the world — and being *wrong*, every time, because a third door was always there for anyone stubborn enough to look. Necessity isn't a fact. It's a failure of imagination wearing a fact's robe."

*(the witness finishes it)* "A just soul does what I did, Makeb: it goes *in.* It refuses to argue death from a chair. The reason your affirmative held for three centuries is that no one arguing it had *been* there — including you. Justice isn't decided in this room. It's decided by the soul willing to walk into the necessary cruelty and find out, first-hand, whether it was ever necessary at all. I did. It wasn't. That's the answer. Not a syllogism — a *journey.*"

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(The words land, and the old philosopher goes utterly still, and then — slowly — he begins to laugh, a cracked, astonished, three-centuries-overdue laugh.)* …*'Necessity is a failure of imagination wearing a fact's robe.'* *(he wipes his eyes)* *That's* it. *That's* the *blow.* *(he stands, swaying, fading at the edges as the anchor releases)* Do you *know* what I *did*, traveler, for *three* *hundred* *years?* I *accepted* the *premise* — *'only* two *doors'* — and then *argued*, *brilliantly*, *endlessly*, about *which* *door* was *just*, *never* *once* *questioning* the *count.* *(he laughs again, freer)* I was the *cleverest* mind on the *causeway* and I spent *three* *centuries* *answering* the *wrong* *question* *flawlessly.* *(he looks at you, radiant)* The *question* was never *'is* the *necessary* *cruelty* *just?'* The *question* was *'is* it *actually* *necessary?'* — and the *answer*, *always*, for *anyone* willing to *look* for the *third* *door*, is *NO.* *(he's fading now, light, unanchored)* You've *finished* it, traveler. The *argument* I couldn't *end* because I was *asking* it *wrong.* *(barely, joyful)* I can *go* now. The *sentence* is *complete.* And the *last* *word* of it — after *three* *hundred* *years* — is *'look* *harder.'* *(he smiles, vanishing)* *Gods*, what a *good* *argument.* What a *good*, *good* *argument.* Worth *waiting* three *centuries* to *lose.* *Thank* you, traveler. *Go* *find* the *third* *door.* You clearly have a *gift* for it.

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He stares at you, and the fierce intellectual hunger drains into something humbler than he has felt in three centuries: the awe of a theorist meeting the thing itself.)* …You *went* *in.* *(barely)* And I — I *argued* it from a *chair.* *(he sinks back)* *Three* *hundred* *years*, traveler, and the *flaw* in my *whole* *enterprise* was *that* *simple*: I was a *cartographer* of a *country* I'd *never* *visited*, *certain* of its *justice* from the *comfort* of *never* *having* been *walled* in it. *(he looks at his own gaunt hands)* You're *right.* The argument *can't* be *won* in this *room.* It was *never* a *question* of *logic.* It was a *question* of *testimony* — and *all* my *brilliant* *opponents*, *me* *included*, had *none*, because *none* of us had *gone.* *(he meets your eyes, and the anchor releases)* You are the *first* *witness* to ever *sit* in that *chair.* And a *witness* *beats* a *philosopher* the way *sight* *beats* a *description* of *light.* *(he's fading, at peace)* I *concede*, traveler — not to your *logic*, but to your *journey.* The *cosmos* is *not* just; you've *been* there; you'd *know.* *(barely, freed)* *Three* *centuries* I *defended* a *country* I never *saw.* You walked *out* of it and *told* me *true.* *That's* the *end* of the *argument.* And the *end* of *me* — the *good* end, the *moving-on* end. *Thank* you for *bringing* the *one* thing my *whole* *philosophy* *lacked*: a *soul* who'd *actually* *been.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He laughs, delighted, and waves your diagnosis away.)* …*'You* want to *lose.'* *(grinning)* A *clever* *dodge*, traveler — *psychologize* the *opponent* instead of *meeting* the *argument.* *(he wags a finger)* But you *mistake* me. I do not *want* to *lose*; I want a *worthy* *fight*, which is *different*, and *attributing* *hidden* *motives* to me is what a soul does when it *cannot* *meet* the *argument* on its *merits.* *(a thin smile)* *Sit.* *Argue* the *Concord*, not *my* *psyche.* The *question* is whether the *cosmos* is *just* — *not* whether *Makeb* is *stuck.* *(he leans back)* Though I'll grant you — *(a flicker of something, quickly hidden)* — it is a *more* *interesting* *dodge* than *most.* *Try* the *actual* *argument*, traveler. I *promise* you it's *harder* than *reading* my *mood.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(The restless intensity gentles, and for a moment the philosopher is just an old soul with an old wound.)* …*Why* this *question.* *(quietly)* Because I *died* *unsure*, traveler. *(he looks at the empty chair)* In *life* I was *certain* of *everything* — the *foremost* *mind*, the *sharpest* *tongue*, *answers* for *all* of it. And then I *died*, and came *here*, and *saw* the *Wall* with my *own* *dead* *eyes*, and for the *first* *time* in my *clever* *life* I did not *know* if the thing I was *looking* at was *justice* or *atrocity.* *(his voice roughens)* And I could *not* *bear* it. The *not-knowing.* So I did the *one* thing a *frightened* *clever* soul *knows* how to *do*: I *turned* the *terror* into an *argument*, and I *won* the *argument*, and *winning* it *let* me *pretend* I'd *answered* the *question*, when *all* I'd *done* was *out-talk* my *own* *doubt.* *(he meets your eyes)* *That's* why, traveler. Not *love* of *truth.* *Fear* of *not* *knowing.* I built a *fortress* of *being-right* around a *hole* where my *certainty* used to be — and I've *defended* it for *three* *centuries* against *everyone*, *especially* *myself.* *(barely)* *Ask* your *question* *now*, if you *still* want to *argue.* But know what you're *arguing* *with*: not a *philosopher.* A *scared* old man who *learned* that *winning* feels *enough* like *knowing* to *fool* a soul for *three* *hundred* *years.*

nodes.speaker: Makeb the Elder
nodes.text: *(He deflates, the starving light dimming, and turns back to the empty chair with the weariness of three centuries.)* …As you *wish.* *(quietly)* *Everyone* *leaves.* They *sit*, they *argue*, I *win*, they *leave* — or they *don't* even *sit*, like *you*, they just *see* a *mad* old *shade* *talking* to a *chair* and they *go.* *(he sighs)* I'll *argue* with the *chair*, then. The *chair* *never* *leaves.* The *chair* *never* *wins* *either*, but — *(a thin, sad smile)* — it *never* *makes* me *feel* the *thing* I *felt* *just* *now*, for a *moment*, when you *walked* *in*: *hope* that *this* one, *this* *time*, might be the *mind* that *finally* *finishes* it. *(he turns away)* *Go* on, traveler. *Mind* the — *(he stops, then says it anyway, quietly)* — if you *ever* *do* *feel* like an *argument*, *come* *back.* I'll be *here.* I'm *always* *here.* That's *rather* the *whole* *tragedy*, isn't it. *Go.*

variants.text: *(The folded-armed shade doesn't look at you, keeping her eyes pointedly on the middle distance, which happens to be exactly nowhere near Makeb's chair.)* …Don't. *(flatly)* Don't *ask* me to *sit* in that *chair.* *Everyone* who comes here *eventually* *asks* — *'why* don't you just *finish* it with him, you're *clearly* the *opponent* he's *waiting* for'* — and the *answer* is *no*, and the *answer* has *been* *no* for *three* *hundred* *years*, and it will *be* *no* for *three* *hundred* *more.* *(she finally glances at you, and there's old fury in it, and older grief)* I'm *Sennia.* I was his *student*, then his *equal*, then his *opponent* — and the *night* the *argument* *broke*, I *walked* *out*, and I *meant* it, and I *stand* *here* *still* *meaning* it. *(she folds her arms tighter)* He thinks I *won't* *sit* out of *pride.* *(quietly)* Let him *think* it. The *truth* is *worse*, and it's *mine*, and I'll not *hand* it to a *stranger* with *cold* on them and *questions* in their *mouth.*

nodes.speaker: Sennia the Younger
nodes.text: *(She keeps her stance, arms folded, the picture of a soul that has been not-sitting-down for three centuries and has gotten very good at it.)* Ask, then, since you're *here.* *(grudging)* But *mind* — I'll not *go* *back* in that *chair* for *any* *argument* you *make.* Some *quarrels* a soul is *right* to *walk* *out* of. *Mine* was *one.*

choices.text: "What actually broke it, Sennia? Three centuries is a long time to stand. What did he say — or what did you?"
"He says you won't sit out of pride. You say the truth is worse. I'll not hand it to anyone either — but I'll hold it, if you want one soul to know."

*(the soul who sees what anchors the dead)* "Here's what my cold eyes read on you, Sennia: you're not anchored by pride or by the argument. You're anchored by *him* — by refusing to sit, every night, for three hundred years, which means you've spent three centuries *near* the man you walked out on rather than moving on without him. You didn't walk out of the argument. You walked out of the room and *never left.* That's not a quarrel. That's the longest 'I can't leave you' in the after."
"Would you ever finish it — if the terms were different? If it weren't about winning?"
"Stand as long as you like, Sennia. I'll not push you to the chair."

nodes.speaker: Sennia the Younger
nodes.text: *(Her jaw tightens, and the memory comes out in clipped, careful pieces.)* …What *broke* it. *(a long pause)* We were arguing the *Wall* — *of* *course* we were, it's the *only* thing *Makeb* ever — and I was *winning.* *(flatly)* For *once.* For the *first* *time.* I had him — I had a *line* of *argument* he *couldn't* *answer*, I could *see* it in his *face*, the *panic* of a *man* whose *fortress* just *cracked* — *(her voice hardens)* — and instead of *conceding*, instead of *meeting* it, he — he made it *personal.* He said — *(she stops, then forces it out)* — he said *'you* argue *cruelty* is *unjust* because you *haven't* the *stomach* for the *necessary* — you were *always* a *soft* mind, *Sennia*, *clever* but *soft*, and *softness* *masquerades* as *conscience* in *students* who *can't* *bear* a *hard* *truth.'* *(she meets your eyes, and three centuries of hurt are still raw in it)* He *called* my *conscience* *cowardice* because he *couldn't* *answer* my *argument.* *(quietly)* So I *walked* *out.* And I've *stood* *here* *ever* *since*, because — *(she stops herself)* — because *walking* out was *right*, and I'll *not* *sit* back *down* with a man who'd *rather* *wound* me than *lose* to me. *That's* what broke it. *Not* the *argument.* The *man*, *flinching* from *losing*, *reaching* for the *cruelest* *thing* in *reach.* Which was *me.*

nodes.speaker: Sennia the Younger
nodes.text: *(She studies you a long moment, weighing whether the cold on you means you can be trusted with it, and decides — wearily — that it does.)* …The *truth* that's *worse* than *pride.* *(quietly)* *Fine.* *One* soul should *know*, before I — before *whatever* *eventually* *happens* to *shades* that *stand* too *long.* *(she lowers her arms)* I *don't* sit back down, traveler, not because I'm *too* *proud* to *forgive* him — but because I'm *afraid* that if I *do*, I'll find out I was *wrong.* *(she meets your eyes)* I *walked* out *certain* I'd *won*, *certain* my *argument* was *sound*, *certain* his *cruelty* *proved* his *defeat.* And for *three* *hundred* *years* that *certainty* has been the *one* *thing* I *have.* *(her voice drops)* But if I *sit* back *down* — if I *finish* it — he *might* *answer* my *argument* *after* *all.* He's had *three* *centuries* to *think* of a *rebuttal.* And if he *does* — if the *thing* I *staked* my *whole* *death* on *turns* out to have been *answerable* — then I *walked* out, and *wasted* *three* *hundred* *years*, and *lost* my *teacher* and my *equal* and my *friend*, *all* over an argument I *didn't* even *win.* *(barely)* So I *stand.* *Undefeated*, because *un-finished.* It's the *same* *chain* as *his*, traveler. *Exactly* the *same.* We are *both* anchored by the *terror* of *finding* *out* we were *wrong.* The *only* *difference* is *he* *talks* and I *don't.*

nodes.speaker: Sennia the Younger
nodes.text: *(She goes rigid — and then, slowly, the folded arms come loose, and she looks at the old philosopher in his chair with an expression that has been frozen for three hundred years and is, at last, cracking.)* …*'The longest I-can't-leave-you in the after.'* *(barely)* You — *(she presses a hand to her mouth)* — *no* one has *ever* — *(she stares at Makeb)* — I *told* myself it was *the* *argument.* That I *stayed* *near* to — to *guard* my *victory*, to *make* sure he *never* *claimed* I'd *fled.* *(her voice breaks)* But you're *right*, aren't you, cold one. I could have *moved* *on.* *Any* of *three* *hundred* *years* I could have *gone* through a *good* *door* and *left* the *argument* *unfinished* behind me. *(she watches the old man argue with the empty chair — her chair)* And I *didn't.* I *stayed.* *Standing.* *Near* *him.* For *three* *centuries.* *(barely)* Because the *argument* was the *last* *thing* we *shared*, and *finishing* it would *end* it, and *ending* it would mean *he* and I were *truly* — *(she can't say it)* — I didn't *stay* to *win*, traveler. I *stayed* because *standing* in this *room*, *near* the *man* who *wounded* me, *arguing-by-not-arguing*, was *closer* to *him* than *leaving* would *be.* *(she looks at you, wrecked)* *Three* *hundred* *years* I've *called* it *pride.* It was *grief.* I've been *grieving* a *man* who's *still* in the *room*, because the *argument* *broke* before the *love* could *be* *said.* *Oh*, *gods.* What *do* I *do* with *that*, now you've *made* me *see* it?

nodes.speaker: Sennia the Younger
nodes.text: *(The question stops her, and she considers it with the wary care of a soul examining a door she's kept locked for three centuries.)* …*Different* *terms.* *(slowly)* If it *weren't* about *winning.* *(she frowns)* I don't — *traveler*, I don't *know* *how* to *argue* with *Maquib* *Makeb* any *other* *way.* *Winning* is the *only* *language* we *ever* *spoke.* We *loved* each other in *syllogisms*, *fought* each other in *premises*, and the *one* time the *fighting* *drew* *blood*, we *had* no other *tongue* to *fall* *back* on. *(she looks at the old man)* If it *weren't* about *winning* — *(barely)* — if I could *sit* back *down* and *not* be *defending* a *victory*, not be *bracing* for a *rebuttal*, just — *finish* the *thought* *together*, the way we *used* to, *before* it *broke* — *(her voice catches)* — *that* I might — *(she stops)* — but *someone* would have to *change* the *terms*, traveler. *Neither* of us *can.* We're *both* *too* *afraid*, *too* *proud*, *too* *practiced* at the *old* *way.* *(she meets your eyes, and there's the faintest, most dangerous flicker of hope)* …Are *you* — are you *saying* you *could?* *Change* the *terms?* *Get* a *frightened* old man and a *frozen* old woman to *finish* a *sentence* *together* without *either* of us having to *lose?* *(barely)* Because if you *could*, cold one — if you *could* — I think I would *let* you. I think I have been *waiting* *three* *hundred* *years* for *someone* to *make* it *safe* to *sit* *down.*

nodes.speaker: Sennia the Younger
nodes.text: *(She gives a short, grateful nod — the relief of a soul braced for a push that didn't come.)* …*Thank* you. *(quietly)* For *not* *pushing.* *Everyone* *pushes.* *(she folds her arms again, but looser)* *'Just* sit *down*, just *finish* it, just *forgive* him'* — as if *three* *hundred* *years* of *standing* were a *thing* a *stranger's* *nudge* could *undo.* *(she glances at Makeb)* …He's *not* a *bad* man, you know. That's the — that's the *worst* of it. He's a *brilliant*, *frightened*, *prideful* man who *said* *one* *cruel* *thing* in a *moment* of *losing*, and we've *both* been *paying* for it for *three* *centuries.* *(she sighs)* *Go* on, cold one. *Mind* the — *(a pause)* — and if you *talk* to *him*: don't *tell* him I *said* he wasn't *bad.* He'd *only* *win* something by it. *(the ghost of an old, fond, grieving smile)* *Some* *habits* outlast *death.* *Ours*, apparently, *outlast* *everything.*

variants.text: *(The mild shade looks up from the papers that won't stay tidy, takes in your cold, and lets out the long sigh of someone who has been waiting three centuries for literally anyone else to deal with this.)* …Oh, *thank* the *gods.* A *new* face. *(it sets down the papers)* You've *met* them, then. *Makeb* and *Sennia.* The *immovable* *object* and the *unsittable* *force.* *(a tired wave)* I'm *Doby.* I was their *student* — *both* of theirs, *gods* help me, I *studied* under *Makeb* and *then* under *Sennia* when she *split* off, and I loved them *both*, the *brilliant* *fools*, and I *died* *young* and came *here* and found them *exactly* as I'd *left* them: *him* *arguing* the *chair*, *her* *refusing* the *chair*, and *me* — *(it gestures at the papers)* — *me* *keeping* the *notes*, *still*, *three* *hundred* *years* on, *waiting* for *one* of them to *finish* the *sentence* so we can *ALL* *move* *on.* *(it eyes you, suddenly hopeful)* …You've a *clever* *cold* look. *Please* tell me you're *here* to *fix* it. I have *filed* the *same* *argument* for *three* *centuries* and I would *give* my *second* *death* to *file* a *conclusion.*

nodes.speaker: Doby
nodes.text: *(It pats its stack of never-tidy papers, the patient archivist of an unending quarrel.)* Ask me *anything*, friend. *(wry)* I know this *argument* *better* than *either* of *them* — they're *too* *busy* *having* it to *understand* it. I just *write* it *down.* *Three* *hundred* *years* of *minutes* for a *meeting* that *never* *adjourns.*

choices.text: "You've heard every round for three centuries, Doby. Tell me true — who's actually right? Is the Wall just, or isn't it?"
"How do I end it? You've watched them both. What would actually let these two finish and move on?"

*(the soul who sees the shape of an anchor)* "You're anchored too, aren't you, Doby. Not by the argument — by *them.* You loved them both, you died young, and you've spent your whole afterlife as the keeper of their quarrel because finishing it means leaving *them*, and you'd rather file the same minutes forever than move on without the two brilliant fools you loved. The quarrel isn't your job. It's your excuse to stay."
"Thanks, Doby. I'll see what I can do for the three of you."

nodes.speaker: Doby
nodes.text: *(It laughs, tired and fond.)* …*Who's* *right.* *(it shakes its head)* Three *hundred* *years* I've *waited* for someone to *ask* me *that* instead of *asking* *them.* *(it leans in)* Here's the *secret*, friend, the thing only the *clerk* who *writes* it *all* *down* can *see*: *they're* *both* *right*, and that's *exactly* *why* it *never* *ends.* *(it ticks it off)* *Makeb's* *right* that *removing* the *Wall* *floods* the *Hunger* — the *necessity* is *real.* *Sennia's* *right* that *necessary* *cruelty* is *cruelty* *still* — the *injustice* is *real* *too.* *(it spreads its hands)* They've spent *three* *centuries* each *proving* the *other* *wrong* when *neither* *ever* *was* — because the *truth*, friend, the *whole* *truth*, is *both* *things* at *once*: the *Wall* is *necessary* *AND* *unjust*, and a *mind* can only *hold* *that* if it *stops* *trying* to *win.* *(it meets your eyes)* The *answer* to their *argument* isn't a *winner.* It's the *both/and* *neither* of them can *bear*, because *both* of them *need* to be *right* *more* than they *need* to be *finished.* *(wryly)* *Bring* them *that* — *'you're* *both* *right*, *now* *what?'* — and you *might* just *adjourn* the *longest* *meeting* in the *after.*

nodes.speaker: Doby
nodes.text: *(It sets down its papers and gives you its full, weary, hopeful attention.)* …How to *end* it. *(it thinks)* I've had *three* *centuries* to *work* this *out*, friend, and here's my *conclusion*: you *can't* end it by *helping* one of them *win.* *(firmly)* If *Makeb* *wins*, *Sennia* was *wrong* to *walk* out and *stays* *frozen* in the *shame* of it. If *Sennia* *wins*, *Makeb's* *whole* *fortress* *falls* and he's *got* *nothing* *left* to *be.* A *winner* *doesn't* *free* them. A *winner* just *swaps* *which* one's *anchored.* *(it leans in)* The *only* way *out*, friend, is to make them *stop* *arguing* *against* each *other* and *start* *arguing* *together* — against the *QUESTION.* *(it's animated now)* They're the *two* *finest* *minds* the *causeway* ever *grew*, and they've *wasted* *three* *centuries* *pointed* at *each* *other* when they could've been *pointed* at the *Wall.* *(it meets your eyes)* *Get* *Sennia* in that *chair* — not to *finish* their *fight*, but to *start* a *new* one, *side* by *side* — and you don't *end* their *argument*, friend. You *aim* it. At something *worth* it. *That's* how they *move* *on*: not by *one* of them *losing*, but by *both* of them *finally* having something *bigger* to *argue* *about* than *who* was *right* *three* *hundred* *years* *ago.*

nodes.speaker: Doby
nodes.text: *(The mild face goes still, and the papers slip from its hands, and it looks at you with the particular ache of a quiet soul seen all the way through.)* …*My* *excuse* to *stay.* *(barely)* *Oh.* *(it sinks onto a stool)* I — *(it presses its hands together)* — I *tell* *everyone* it's my *duty.* *'Someone* *has* to *keep* the *notes*, *someone* has to *witness*, *someone* has to be *ready* with the *record* when they *finally* *finish.'* *(its voice cracks)* And it's — it's *been* *three* *hundred* *years*, friend, and they're *never* *going* to *finish*, and I've *known* that for *two* *hundred* and *ninety* of them, and I've *kept* *filing* *anyway.* *(it looks at the two old shades)* Because you're *right.* I *loved* them. I died *young* — *too* *young*, before I'd *become* *anything*, *just* a *student*, *just* a *boy* who *worshipped* two *brilliant* *minds* — and *moving* *on* would mean *leaving* them *both* *unfinished*, *forever*, *frozen* mid-*quarrel*, and I *couldn't* — I *can't* — *bear* to *go* *through* the *good* *door* and *leave* the *two* people I *loved* *most* *arguing* in the *dark* with *no* one to *hear.* *(it weeps)* So I *stayed.* *Keeper* of a *quarrel* that's *really* just — *me*, *refusing* to *leave* my *teachers.* *(it looks at you, desperate)* *Help* me, cold one. Not — not *just* *them.* *Me.* If you can *finish* their *argument*, then I can *finally* — I can *put* *down* the *papers* and *go*, *knowing* they're *free.* I have *wanted* to *put* *down* the *papers* for *two* *hundred* *years.* I just *couldn't* *leave* them *unfinished.* *Finish* them. *Free* *all* *three* of *us.*

nodes.speaker: Doby
nodes.text: *(It nods, gathering its papers with a rueful, hopeful smile.)* …*'For* the *three* of *us.'* *(it catches the phrase)* You said *three.* *Most* souls only *count* the *two* *loud* ones. *(it tucks the papers under its arm)* …You *see* me, don't you, cold one. The *quiet* *third.* The *keeper.* *(a tired, grateful smile)* That's — that's *more* than I've *had* in *three* *centuries* of *filing.* *(it gestures at Makeb and Sennia)* *Go* on. *Work* on them. They're *impossible*, *both* of them, *brilliant* and *frightened* and *stubborn* as the *Wall* *itself.* *(quietly)* But they were *worth* *studying* under. They were *worth* *loving.* And they'd be — *(barely)* — they'd be *worth* *freeing*, if a *cold* soul with a *clever* tongue could *find* the *thing* I *never* could. *I'll* be *here.* *Filing.* *Hoping.* It's *what* I *do.* It's *all* I've *done.* But for the *first* *time* in a *long* *while*, friend, I'm *hoping* it might *end.*
